design_philosophy.core_loop: Select Robot Master stage → navigate side-scrolling level → defeat boss → acquire Master Weapon → use weapon to access new stages or exploit weaknesses → progress to next tier of Robot Masters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Execution-based combat requiring pattern recognition and precise platforming; weapon scarcity creates resource tension; weakness-chain puzzle (determining which weapon defeats which boss)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a versatile robot hero who adapts to enemies by stealing their powers and using those abilities strategically; gradual empowerment through weapon collection
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Strictly adherent to NES formula despite SNES capabilities; rejects innovation in favor of proven systems. Gated Robot Master selection (4 selectable initially, 4 unlocked later) enforces a specific progression path. Introduction of bolt currency and shop system ('Eddie's Cybernetic Support Shop') shifts from pure skill to resource management hybrid. Refuses to innovate like contemporary Mega Man X series, prioritizing franchise stability over experimentation.

difficulty.lives_system: Standard extra lives earned from defeated enemies; lives persist across level attempts
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpointing; boss battles are final stage of each level with no mid-level checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency through weapon selection strategy. Players can purchase power-ups and items using bolts earned in levels, mitigating difficulty. Rush Super Adapter (powerful combination tool) can be unlocked via R-U-S-H letter collection, providing skill-based difficulty reduction. Specific Master Weapon weaknesses mean optimal weapon choice dramatically impacts boss difficulty.

progression.upgrade_system: Master Weapon acquisition from defeated Robot Masters; special power-ups (Beat bird helper, Proto Man shield); Rush Super Adapter combination tool; bolts currency system enabling shop purchases of consumables and permanent upgrades
progression.unlock_mechanism: Initial 4 Robot Master stages (Freeze Man, Junk Man, Burst Man, Cloud Man) selectable; defeating these unlocks access to remaining 4 stages (Spring Man, Slash Man, Shade Man, Turbo Man). Two-tier gate enforces progression order. Final stages require prior weapons.
replayability_hooks: Hidden R-U-S-H letters scattered in levels for Super Adapter unlock
Rush Search ability to dig up items encourages revisiting locations
Secret fighting game mode (two-player versus) unlocked via end-game password
Multiple weapon options per stage encourage different approach attempts
Bolt farming for shop upgrades incentivizes level replaying

world_building_techniques: Opening cutscene: Mega Man driven into ruined city by Roll and Auto, establishing stakes visually
NPC introductions: Bass and Treble mysterious rival robots with unclear allegiance (plot twist later reveals betrayal)
Environmental storytelling: Ruined cityscape shows Dr. Wily's 6-month prison absence consequences
Character encounters: In-level meeting with injured Bass at Dr. Light's lab signals relationship development
Weapon-get cutscenes: Localized regional variations suggest narrative customization per region
Level design implicitly tells Robot Master stories through stage aesthetics (Junk Man's dumping grounds, Freeze Man's icy terrain, Cloud Man's sky platforms)

boss_design.philosophy: Robot Masters are specialized combat puzzles emphasizing weakness exploitation over raw difficulty. Each master represents a themed ability (Freeze Man = ice, Junk Man = debris, etc.) and is designed around specific Master Weapon vulnerability. Boss encounters reward pattern recognition and strategic weapon selection over mechanical execution. Eight Robot Masters split into two unlockable tiers encourages staged progression and prevents early overwhelming.
notable_bosses: Burst Man (first-tier Robot Master; fires bomb projectiles; weak to specific Master Weapon)
Freeze Man (first-tier; ice-based attacks; environmental hazard integration)
Junk Man (stage features spinning drum obstacles; master of debris weaponry)
Cloud Man (sky-based stage with platform challenges)
Shade Man (second-tier; petrification mechanics; unused 'appearance' animation suggests scrapped petrify-aura effect)
Bass with Treble (recurring rival; boss encounter before final Wily stages; fusion state with special animation potential noted as unused)

faction_indicators: Mega Man (hero, allied with Roll and Auto, repairs at Dr. Light's lab)
Dr. Wily (imprisoned antagonist; orchestrates robot liberation from captivity via backup robots)
Bass and Treble (mysterious robot pair; claim to oppose Wily but betray Mega Man; represent unaligned faction with own agenda)
Dr. Light's faction (support network providing repairs and equipment)
Eight Robot Masters (freed by Wily's contingency plan; serve as antagonist army)
danger_signals: City-wide ruins indicate catastrophic attack scale and civilian impact
Autonomous robot activation (6-month timer suggests premeditated prison escape contingency)
Bass and Treble's betrayal indicates infiltration/double-agent danger within alliance structure
Robot Masters' coordinated rampage suggests organized resistance to capture, not mindless chaos
Wily's successful prison escape despite imprisonment demonstrates threat persistence
environmental_storytelling: Ruined urban landscape establishes severity and scale of Robot Master rampage
Stage-specific aesthetics embed master identity (ice caverns for Freeze Man, junk yards for Junk Man, cloud platforms for Cloud Man)
Dr. Light's lab as refuge sanctuary establishes institutional opposition to Wily
Road journey opening (Roll, Auto, Mega Man driving through destruction) localizes global threat to specific city geography
Unused Bunby Met and rubble graphics suggest original concept of environmental destruction/debris scattered by machine assault

sources_used: tcrf - Mega Man 7: The Cutting Room Floor (development notes, unused assets, engine reuse confirmation, 3-month development cycle)
wikipedia - Mega Man 7 article (gameplay mechanics, plot summary, Robot Master structure, character roles, development team credits)
